section.title: Slaves to Righteousness
section.sectionRationale: Paul presses the same question from a new angle: if we are under grace and not law, shall we sin? Again he answers no, and shows why through the picture of slavery. Everyone is a slave, either of sin, which leads to death, or of obedience, which leads to righteousness. Believers have been set free from sin and enslaved to God, and the fruit of that new slavery is holiness and its end eternal life. The passage rises to one of the most famous verses in the Bible: the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.
section.thesis: There is no neutral freedom; everyone serves a master, either sin that pays the wage of death or God who gives the free gift of eternal life, and believers, once slaves of sin, have been set free and enslaved to righteousness, bearing the fruit of holiness whose end is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the question renewed and the law of slavery, you are slaves of the one you obey; the great exchange, once slaves of sin, now set free and enslaved to righteousness and to God; and the two harvests contrasted, the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ.

units.label: Slaves of the one you obey
units.note: no neutral freedom, either sin or obedience

verses.text: What then? Shall we sin because we are not under law, but under grace? Certainly not!

Do you not know that when you offer yourselves as obedient {{doulos|slaves}}, you are slaves to the one you obey, whether you are slaves to sin leading to death, or to obedience leading to righteousness?

units.label: Set free from sin, enslaved to God

verses.text: But thanks be to God that, though you once were slaves to sin, you wholeheartedly obeyed the form of teaching to which you were committed.
You have been set free from {{hamartia|sin}} and have become slaves to righteousness.

I am speaking in human terms because of the weakness of your flesh. Just as you used to offer the parts of your body in slavery to impurity and to escalating wickedness, so now offer them in slavery to righteousness leading to holiness.

units.label: The two harvests
units.note: the wages of sin, the gift of God

verses.text: For when you were slaves to sin, you were free of obligation to righteousness.
What fruit did you reap at that time from the things of which you are now ashamed? The outcome of those things is death.
But now that you have been set free from sin and have become slaves to God, the fruit you reap leads to {{hagiasmos|holiness}}, and the outcome is eternal life.
For the {{opsonion|wages}} of sin is death, but the {{charisma|gift}} of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

doulos.gloss: A slave or bondservant, one wholly owned by another and bound to obey. Paul uses this stark image to show there is no neutral autonomy: a person belongs either to sin or to God. To be enslaved is to be owned, directed, and answerable to a master, and the whole question is which master one serves and where that service leads.

hamartia.gloss: Sin, portrayed by Paul as a slave master who owns and rules those under it. To have been set free from sin is to have been released from that ownership and transferred to a new master. Sin is not a light habit but a tyrant that claims obedience and pays its wage in death.

hagiasmos.gloss: Sanctification or holiness, the process and state of being set apart for God and progressively conformed to His will. It is the fruit that grows from the new slavery to righteousness. Holiness is not the ground of acceptance but the harvest of a life now owned by God, and its end is eternal life.

opsonion.gloss: Wages, the pay given to a soldier for service rendered. Paul says the wages of sin is death: death is what sin duly earns and pays out, a just recompense owed for service to sin. The word underlines that death is not an accident but the settled wage that sin's slavery deserves.

charisma.gloss: A free gift, a gift of grace. Paul deliberately contrasts it with wages: eternal life is not earned like a wage but given freely, out of God's grace, in Christ Jesus. Where sin pays what is owed, God gives what could never be deserved, and the gift is life without end in His Son.

basic.context: Paul has said believers are not under law but under grace. Someone might twist that to mean we are free to sin. Paul says no again, and explains with a picture everyone in the ancient world understood: slavery. You are always a slave of whatever you obey. Either you serve sin, which pays out death, or you serve God, which leads to righteousness, holiness, and eternal life.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the passage as an extended metaphor of two slaveries. Paul even apologizes for the human picture, speaking in human terms, but uses it because it makes the point vivid: there is no neutral middle ground. Trace the two paths side by side, sin to death and obedience to life, and notice how the whole passage climaxes in verse 23, the great contrast of wages earned and gift given.
basic.originalLanguages: The controlling image is the doulos, the slave who is wholly owned. Believers were once owned by hamartia, sin, but have been set free and now serve God, and the fruit is hagiasmos, holiness. The climax contrasts opsonion, the wage sin pays, which is death, with charisma, the free gift of God, which is eternal life in Christ Jesus.
basic.typology: The exodus from slavery in Egypt into service of the LORD stands behind Paul's picture: God redeems His people not into autonomy but into glad service of Himself. The transfer from one master to another fulfills the pattern of ransom and redemption, where a price is paid so that slaves change hands and belong now to their Redeemer.
basic.christ: The passage ends in Christ. Eternal life is the gift of God in Christ Jesus our Lord; it is found in Him and given through Him. He is the Redeemer who sets slaves of sin free and brings them under the gentle lordship of God. The whole contrast between death earned and life given is resolved in Him, for it is His death and resurrection that purchase the gift.
basic.god: God is the new master to whom believers now belong, and the giver of the free gift of eternal life. Paul breaks into thanks to God that those once enslaved to sin now obey from the heart. God does not merely release sinners; He claims them for Himself and grants them a life and an end that sin could never provide.
basic.crossReferences: Romans 6:1-14 grounds this in union with Christ's death and resurrection. John 8 says everyone who sins is a slave of sin, but the Son sets free indeed. Galatians 5 contrasts the fruit of the flesh and the fruit of the Spirit. Romans 8 declares no condemnation and life in the Spirit for those in Christ.
basic.oneStory: The Bible tells of a humanity enslaved to sin since Adam, and of a God who redeems slaves for Himself, first from Egypt, finally from sin itself. Here Paul shows the great exchange at the heart of that story: sin pays out death, but God gives the free gift of eternal life in His Son, transferring His people from one master to another forever.

context.literary: This section parallels verses 1 to 14, raising the objection again, shall we sin, and answering it, this time under the image of slavery rather than death and resurrection. It moves through a renewed question, an extended metaphor of two masters, a look back at the barren fruit of the old life, and a climactic aphorism in verse 23 that gathers the whole chapter into a single sentence.
context.historical: Slavery was pervasive in the Roman world, so Paul's readers knew exactly what it meant to be owned and bound to obey a master. He draws on that daily reality, while noting he speaks in human terms, to make plain that no one is truly autonomous. The form of teaching to which they were committed likely refers to the pattern of apostolic instruction they received at conversion.
context.canonical: Romans 6:23 is among the most quoted verses in Scripture, a compact statement of the gospel: sin earns death, God gives eternal life in Christ. The chapter as a whole is foundational for the doctrine of sanctification, showing that those justified by grace are freed from sin's mastery and bound to serve righteousness.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To answer once more the charge that grace licenses sin, by showing that freedom from law is not freedom for sin but a change of masters: believers have been set free from sin and enslaved to God, and their new service bears the fruit of holiness whose end is eternal life in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul describes the objective change of ownership that has taken place, once slaves of sin, now slaves of God, and prescribes the fitting response: now offer the parts of your body in slavery to righteousness leading to holiness. The command rests on the accomplished transfer from one master to another.

debates.question: Is death in this passage merely physical, or something deeper?

views.label: The full penalty of sin, culminating in eternal death
views.case: The historic reading takes death as the whole wage of sin, physical and spiritual, ending in eternal separation from God, set in contrast to eternal life; the outcome of sin's slavery is comprehensive ruin.

views.label: Only bodily death in this life
views.case: Some limit it to physical death; but the deliberate contrast with eternal life shows Paul means far more than the grave, the final and eternal consequence of sin.

debates.bearing: Reading death as the full and final wage of sin heightens the gospel: what sinners have earned is total ruin, yet God freely gives eternal life in Christ, a gift infinitely beyond anything deserved.

debates.question: How does slavery to God relate to Christian freedom?

views.label: True freedom is glad slavery to God
views.case: The historic reading holds that being enslaved to God is the only real freedom, for it frees from sin's tyranny and death; freedom and glad service to God are not opposites but the same reality in Christ.

views.label: Slavery language is only a temporary rhetorical device
views.case: Some treat the slavery image as a concession Paul quickly drops; yet while he does note he speaks in human terms, the reality he describes, being owned by God, is his settled and joyful conviction.

debates.bearing: Seeing freedom as glad service to God guards against both legalism and license: the believer is not autonomous but joyfully belongs to God, and in that belonging finds life rather than the death sin's freedom brings.

study.originalLanguages: The passage is built on the image of the doulos, the slave who is wholly owned and bound to obey; Paul insists there is no third option beyond serving sin or serving God. Believers were once owned by hamartia, sin, but have been set free and transferred to God, and the harvest of this new ownership is hagiasmos, holiness or sanctification, a life progressively set apart for God. The climax turns on two contrasting words: opsonion, the wage that sin earns and pays out, which is death, and charisma, the free gift of God, which could never be earned, eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

typology.fulfillment: Israel redeemed from bondage in Egypt to serve the LORD foreshadows the believer set free from slavery to sin to serve God.
The transfer of a slave through a price paid points to Christ, who purchases sinners from sin's ownership to belong to God.
The two harvests of death and of eternal life fulfill the pattern that what is sown determines what is reaped, resolved in the fruit of holiness borne by those who belong to God.

study.christ: The whole passage climbs toward Christ in its final phrase: the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord. Eternal life is located in Him and given through Him; He is the Redeemer who purchases slaves of sin and brings them under God's gracious lordship. The contrast between the wage of death that sin pays and the free gift of life that God gives is made possible only by His death and resurrection, so that the great exchange of masters is founded entirely on His finished work.
study.god: God stands at the center as the new master to whom the redeemed now belong and as the giver of eternal life. Paul cannot describe the change without breaking into thanks to God. God does not leave sinners in the ruin their slavery earns; He sets them free, claims them as His own, and grants them a fruit and an end, holiness now and eternal life at the last, that sin could never yield. The passage exalts a God whose grace both liberates and gives.

crossReferences.note: The believer's death to sin and new life in union with Christ, grounding the change of masters described here.
Everyone who sins is a slave of sin, but if the Son sets you free, you are free indeed.
The works of the flesh contrasted with the fruit of the Spirit, paralleling the two harvests.
No condemnation in Christ, and the law of the Spirit of life setting free from the law of sin and death.

study.oneStory: Since Adam, humanity has been enslaved to sin, and the wage of that slavery is death. But the Bible tells of a God who redeems slaves for Himself, bringing Israel out of Egypt and finally bringing sinners out of bondage to sin through His Son. Here that story reaches a hinge: the great exchange of masters. Sin pays out death, but God gives the free gift of eternal life in Christ Jesus, so that those once owned by sin now belong forever to God and bear the fruit of holiness.

quiz.q: According to Paul, what are the only two options for whom a person can serve?

options.text: Either sin, leading to death, or obedience, leading to righteousness.
options.note: You are a slave of the one you obey; there is no neutral ground.

options.text: Either sin or complete personal autonomy.
options.note: Close, but Paul denies any true autonomy; the alternative to serving sin is serving God.

options.text: Either the law of Moses or the Roman law.
options.note: The contrast is between slavery to sin and slavery to God, not two legal codes.

options.text: Either sin now or obedience later, with a neutral middle.
options.note: There is no neutral middle; one is always a slave of some master.

quiz.q: What has happened to believers, according to verse 18?

options.text: They have been set free from sin and have become slaves to righteousness.
options.note: A real transfer of ownership from sin to righteousness.

options.text: They have become completely free of any master at all.
options.note: Freedom from sin means new service to God, not autonomy.

options.text: They remain slaves to sin but are now forgiven.
options.note: Believers are truly forgiven, but Paul says more: they are actually set free from sin's mastery, not still enslaved.

options.text: They have earned their freedom by good works.
options.note: The freedom is God's gift in Christ, not earned by works.

quiz.q: What fruit does the new slavery to God produce?

options.text: Holiness, whose outcome is eternal life.
options.note: The fruit is sanctification and the end is eternal life.

options.text: A sense of pride in one's own achievement.
options.note: The fruit is holiness given by God, not self-congratulation.

options.text: Freedom to live however one pleases.
options.note: The fruit is holiness, not license; that is the opposite of the point.

options.text: Growth in holiness, though its final outcome remains uncertain.
options.note: The fruit truly is holiness, but Paul is clear its outcome is certain, eternal life.

quiz.q: What is the meaning of the wages of sin is death?

options.text: Death is the just recompense that sin earns and pays out.
options.note: Wages are what is owed for service; sin's service earns death.

options.text: Death is an accident that has nothing to do with sin.
options.note: Paul says death is precisely the wage sin pays, not an accident.

options.text: Only especially wicked sins earn any penalty.
options.note: The wage belongs to sin as such, not only to extreme sins.

options.text: Death is merely the end of physical life with no deeper loss.
options.note: Death here includes physical death, but the contrast with eternal life shows it means far more, the full and final penalty.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe eternal life in verse 23?

options.text: The free gift of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.
options.note: Eternal life is a gift of grace, not a wage, given in Christ.

options.text: A wage earned by a lifetime of obedience.
options.note: Paul deliberately calls it a gift, not a wage, to exclude earning.

options.text: A reward owed to those who keep the law.
options.note: It is a free gift of grace, not something owed for law keeping.

options.text: Something every person receives automatically.
options.note: It rightly sees eternal life as a gift, but Paul locates it in Christ Jesus, received in union with Him, not given automatically to all.

questions: Paul says there is no neutral freedom; we are always slaves of some master. Whom are you actually serving in the daily choices of your life?
Believers have been set free from sin and enslaved to God. How does seeing yourself as owned by God, rather than autonomous, reshape your view of obedience?
Paul asks what fruit you reaped from the things of which you are now ashamed. Looking back, how has sin's promised freedom actually paid out in your life?
The fruit of belonging to God is holiness leading to eternal life. Where is God calling you to offer some part of your life in slavery to righteousness this week?
The wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ. How does the contrast between what you have earned and what God freely gives move you to worship?

translationNotes.note: The form of teaching to which you were committed likely refers to the pattern of apostolic instruction received at conversion; some render it the mold into which you were handed over, picturing believers shaped by the gospel they obeyed.

I am speaking in human terms because of the weakness of your flesh signals that Paul knows the slavery image is imperfect for describing service to God, yet he uses it to make the reality vivid and inescapable.
Wages renders opsonion, a soldier's pay, what is duly owed; it is deliberately contrasted with gift, charisma, a gift of grace, so that death is earned but eternal life is freely given in Christ.
